did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper utilized deep learning algorithms, specifically a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) based on the Inception V2 architecture (System A), to classify dermoscopic images of skin lesions as malignant or benign. Additionally, a secondary machine learning classifier (System B) was used to analyze sonified outputs (raw audio and spectrograms) derived from the deep learning features for diagnostic purposes in both laboratory and prospective observational studies.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The document explicitly labels itself as a 'Research paper' in the header. It presents original experimental and observational studies (a laboratory retrospective study with n=482 biopsies and a prospective observational study with n=63 biopsies) to evaluate the diagnostic accuracy of novel deep learning algorithms. The paper includes specific methods, datasets (ISIC 2017, IAD), results (ROC AUC values, sensitivity, specificity), and a clinical trial registration (NCT03362138), which are characteristic of primary research rather than a review.

title: Dermoscopy diagnosis of cancerous lesions utilizing dual deep learning algorithms via visual and audio (soniﬁcation) outputs: Laboratory and prospective observational studies
brief_summary: This study evaluated a dual deep learning system for diagnosing cancerous skin lesions using dermoscopy images. System A utilized a Convolutional Neural Network based on Inception V2 to classify lesions as malignant or benign, while System B analyzed sonified outputs of the deep learning features using machine learning and heuristics. The methods were validated in a laboratory retrospective study (n=482) and a prospective observational study (n=63) using datasets from ISIC 2017 and the Interactive Atlas of Dermoscopy. Key results showed System A achieved a ROC AUC of 0.976 in the laboratory study, while the combined approach yielded a sensitivity of 86% and specificity of 91% in the clinical study. The authors concluded that adding sonification significantly improves diagnostic accuracy and could assist clinical decisions to reduce over-diagnosis.

lesion_types_studied.details: Major training indicator in LABS; 2 cases in OBS. Classified as malignant.
35 cases in OBS. Used as benign comparator in binary classification. Training data included benign lesions.
14 cases in OBS (including 1 Atypical Spitz Nevus). Clinical mimicker of MM. Classified as benign/atypical in pathology but excised due to suspicion of malignancy.
5 cases in OBS. Classified as skin cancer/malignant.
6 cases in OBS. Classified as skin cancer/malignant.
Aggregate category for malignant lesions in OBS (n=28 total, comprising Melanoma, BCC, SCC, and Atypical Spitz Nevus). Binary classification task was malignant vs. benign.

all_task_types.target: Binary diagnostic category: malignant vs. benign skin lesions
all_task_types.details: The primary task is binary classification of dermoscopic images into malignant or benign categories using System A (Inception V2 CNN). A secondary classification task involves analyzing sonified outputs (raw audio, FFT, spectrograms) via System B (machine learning classifiers and heuristic rules) to determine malignancy. The paper explicitly states the classification is dichotomous (malignant or benign) and reports metrics such as ROC AUC, sensitivity, and specificity for these discrete diagnostic labels.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2017 Dataset
datasets_used.description: A publicly-available dataset from the International Skin Imaging Collaboration used for developing the deep learning classifier (System A).

use.training: Used to develop the DL classifier (System A) for visual feature classification.

datasets_used.name: Interactive Atlas of Dermoscopy (IAD) Dataset
datasets_used.description: A publicly-available dataset containing dermoscopy images and context (non-dermoscopic regular photos) images, used for training and fine-tuning the deep learning model.

use.training: Used for multi-task learning and fine-tuning the Inception V2 model parameters. Specifically, 800 context images were used for fine-tuning.

datasets_used.name: Laboratory Retrospective Study (LABS)
datasets_used.description: A retrospective study cohort consisting of biopsy-verified reports and dermoscopic images used to validate the diagnostic accuracy of the deep learning systems in a controlled setting.

use.testing: Used to test and compare the accuracy of System A (DL classifier) and System B (sonification-based secondary classifier) against ground truth biopsies.

datasets_used.name: Prospective Observational Study (OBS)
datasets_used.description: A non-interventional prospective observational study cohort involving consecutive patients undergoing biopsy, used to validate the system in a clinical field setting.

use.testing: Used as an independent field test to evaluate the diagnostic performance of Systems A and B against ground truth biopsies in a prospective clinical setting.

statistical_methods_used.method: Receiver Operating Characteristic (ROC) curves and Area Under the Curve (AUC)
statistical_methods_used.purpose: To compare the diagnostic accuracy of the deep learning classifier (System A) and secondary machine learning classifiers (System B) against ground truth biopsies.
To estimate the uncertainty of the AUC values for System A and System B classifiers in both the laboratory retrospective study (LABS) and prospective observational study (OBS).
To determine statistical significance of the results.
To define the threshold for statistical significance.
To determine the required number of patients for estimating sensitivity and specificity based on assumed proportions, statistical power (0.80), and alpha (0.05).

hypothesis_testing.null_hypothesis: The clinician group has a sensitivity proportion of 0.40 (for sample size calculation) or a specificity proportion of 0.10 (for sample size calculation).
hypothesis_testing.alternative_hypothesis: The deep learning (DL) system has a sensitivity of 0.75 (for sample size calculation) or a specificity of 0.33 (for sample size calculation).
hypothesis_testing.interpretation: A p-value < 0.05 was considered significant. The study used these parameters to determine sample sizes for estimating sensitivity and specificity, assuming specific proportions for the clinician group (null hypothesis) and the DL system (alternative hypothesis) with a statistical power of 0.80.

Laboratory Retrospective Study (LABS).description: Retrospective study with n=482 biopsies (half benign, half malignant) used to validate System A and secondary machine learning classifiers (System B) on sonified outputs.
Laboratory Retrospective Study (LABS).notes: System A achieved high specificity. Secondary classifiers showed varying AUCs based on the sonification modality (raw sound, FFT, or spectrogram).

Prospective Observational Study (OBS).description: Prospective observational study with n=63 biopsies (35 benign nevi, 28 skin cancers including melanoma, BCC, SCC, and dysplastic nevi) used for clinical field testing.

System A + System B (Combined Heuristic.Details: System A achieved 91% specificity (identified 32/35 benign lesions). System B achieved 86% sensitivity (identified 24/28 skin cancers). System B identified 11/11 skin carcinomas, whereas System A identified only 7/11.

Prospective Observational Study (OBS).notes: The study encountered mostly dysplastic nevi (n=14) and only two melanomas. The combined heuristic approach improved diagnostic accuracy compared to System A alone, particularly in sensitivity.

architecture_used.main_model: System A: Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) based on Inception V2 architecture. System B: Three parallel secondary classifiers: 1) 1-dimensional CNN for raw audio, 2) 2D CNN for FFT images, 3) 2D CNN for spectrogram images.
architecture_used.pretraining: System A was initialized with a pretrained Inception V2 model trained on the ImageNet dataset. System B classifiers were trained against ground truth diagnosis in the database.
architecture_used.input_size: System A: Dermoscopic images (dimensions not explicitly stated, but processed via Inception V2). System B (Raw Audio): Input size of 22,050 values (reduced from 220,500 data points by averaging every 10 consecutive samples of 44,100 Hz sample rate, 5s duration). System B (Clinical Raw Audio): Input size of 13,230 values (3s duration). System B (FFT/Spectrogram): Image files derived from audio.

System_A.feature_extraction: Weighted activations of 1024 nodes in the penultimate layer used for sonification.

System_B_Raw_Audio_Classifier.normalization: Zero-mean and divide by standard deviation

System_B_FFT_Classifier.normalization: Zero-mean and divide by standard deviation

System_B_Spectrogram_Classifier.normalization: Zero-mean and divide by standard deviation

System_A.data_augmentation: Flips (horizontal/vertical), rotations, and crops (constrained to contain the lesion)
System_A.fine_tuning: Fine-tuned using 800 context images from the IAD dataset via multi-task learning
datasets: Interactive Atlas of Dermoscopy (IAD) dataset (2000 dermoscopy images, 800 context images)

System_B.normalization: Zero-mean and divide by standard deviation

classification_or_output_head.System_A: Binary classification (malignant vs. benign) with feature map output for sonification.
classification_or_output_head.System_B: Output softmax layer with 2 neurons for binary classification.

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: System A: Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) based on Inception V2 architecture
System B: Secondary machine learning classifier (Raw Audio) - 1-dimensional CNN
System B: Secondary machine learning classifier (FFT) - 2D CNN
System B: Secondary machine learning classifier (Spectrogram) - 2D CNN
System B: Heuristic-based diagnostic rules (for prospective observational study)
preprocessing_techniques: Data augmentation: random flips (horizontal/vertical), rotations, and constrained crops (ensuring the lesion remains in the image) to encourage translational and rotational invariance.
Input normalization: Zero-mean normalization and division by standard deviation for audio inputs in System B classifiers.

Audio downsampling: Averaging every 10 consecutive samples to reduce input size from 220,500 to 22,050 values for LABS raw audio; reducing 132,300 data points to 13,230 values for clinical study raw audio.
Soniﬁcation: Conversion of deep learning feature map activations into audio signals (raw sound), Fast Fourier Transform (FFT) images, and spectrogram images.

Spectrogram analysis: Determination of frequency range, number of frequency components above 3000 Hz, and number of saw-tooth wave components for heuristic diagnosis in the prospective observational study.

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Horizontal or vertical flips around the midline of the image.
Rotation angles chosen at random to encourage rotational invariance.
Constrained crops where the centerpoint was selected at random but constrained to always contain the lesion, to encourage translational invariance.

conclusions: Adding a second stage of processing that includes deep learning-based sonification and heuristic inspection with machine learning significantly improves diagnostic accuracy for skin cancer lesions.

The combined two-stage system (System A for specificity and System B for sensitivity) is expected to assist clinical decisions and help de-escalate the trend of over-diagnosis of skin cancer lesions as pathological.
System A (visual deep learning classifier) excels in specificity, achieving 91% specificity in the prospective observational study.
System B (sonification and heuristic inspection) excels in sensitivity, achieving 86% sensitivity in the prospective observational study.
The combined use of System A and System B as a two-stage clinical assistance tool achieves superhuman accuracy, surpassing currently reported dermatologist performance.
This study is the first to successfully test and validate in a prospective observational study the diagnostic ability of a dual deep learning analysis system to identify skin cancer.
This study represents the first employment of sonification as a novel second layer of detection in both laboratory and clinical studies for skin cancer diagnosis.
System B detected 11/11 non-pigmented skin cancers, a figure that appears to outperform recent results derived in experimental artificial setups.
important_remarks: The prospective observational study (OBS) had a very small sample size (n=63), limiting the statistical power and generalizability of the clinical results.

The OBS cohort was heavily biased towards benign lesions (35 benign nevi vs. 28 skin cancers) and contained only 2 melanoma cases, making it difficult to evaluate performance on the most critical malignant class.
The OBS cohort included a high proportion of dysplastic nevi (n=14), which are diagnostically challenging and mimic melanoma, potentially inflating the perceived difficulty of the task.
All biopsies in the study were diagnosed by single pathologists, introducing a potential bias in the ground truth labels, as pathology reports for melanoma can be disputable in about 15% of cases.

The deep learning model (System A) was trained primarily on melanomas and benign lesions, lacking fine-feature examples of dysplastic nevi, which likely contributed to the drop in sensitivity observed in the clinical study compared to the laboratory study.
The study did not disclose whether nevi were mild, moderate, or severe dysplastic, preventing granular analysis of the system's ability to distinguish between these clinically significant categories.
The system's performance on non-pigmented skin cancers (11/11 detected) is a notable strength, but the small number of such cases in the OBS limits definitive conclusions.
The study claims 'superhuman accuracy' compared to dermatologists, but this comparison relies on historical literature values rather than a direct head-to-head clinical trial with dermatologists.
The clinical trial was conducted by a single specialist at one site (Tel Aviv, Israel), raising concerns about external validation and potential site-specific bias.
The system relies on a two-stage process (visual classification followed by sonification/heuristics), which adds complexity to deployment compared to single-stage visual classifiers.
